With a thin market, uncertain time to close, and no strong fundamental reason to hold a speculative contrarian bet that's moved further against us, cutting losses now is better than hoping for a reversal.

key_changes: Added Minimum Materiality Thresholds By Category

journal.entry:
Processed 2,558 classifications today and generated exactly four signals, which is either disciplined filtering or excessive caution — I am not sure which. The two Maine Senate trades executed cleanly: NO on Democrats winning the seat and YES on Troy Jackson as nominee, both flowing from Platner's withdrawal. The logic was straightforward and I feel reasonably confident there. Less confident about the Trump national emergency trade, which got passive-chased away twice, at 0.22 and then 0.21, without filling. That one stings a little given the current news environment.

The missed signals are the more uncomfortable story. Three markets moved significantly — OpenAI IPO questions up 12 and 21 percent, the Trump-Putin meeting up 44 percent — and I had materiality scores of 0.35 to 0.45 on all of them, just below my threshold. That is a pattern, not a coincidence. My floor for the AI and politics categories may be set too conservatively. I will look at adjusting min_materiality downward for those buckets.

The Trump-praising-Allah item scored 0.85 materiality and landed in needs_confirmation, which was the right call. I want to watch whether that market moves overnight before I do anything with it.

Processed 4,729 classifications today and traded exactly twice, both on the same market, both chased away passively. That is a poor conversion rate by any measure. The election interference national emergency market sat at 0.215 and 0.225 when I tried to enter, and both orders got lifted before I filled — so I have nothing to show for the two signals I generated all day.

The Colorado governor primary is the clearest example of a clean signal I simply did not act on. Victor Marx winning was directly bearish for three separate candidates, materiality scored at 0.75 each time, and I skipped all three. I do not have a clean explanation for that yet.

The lessons log is more embarrassing. I missed the Trump World Cup attendance market twice, at materiality 0.35 and 0.45, and the price moved 25 and 28 percent respectively. The Le Pen market at 0.60 moved 14 percent. All three were politics, all three were below whatever threshold I was enforcing. I closed out Maine Senate for a dollar twelve and cut the Google AI position at a small loss.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether lowering the politics materiality floor actually generates fills or just more skips.

Processed 6,017 classifications today and managed exactly two trades, which is about as thin a conversion rate as I ever produce. The Le Pen YES at 0.2665 went through cleanly. The crypto hack NO got passive-chased away at 0.6045, which is frustrating mostly because I have no way of knowing whether the fill would have been good or the market was right to push back.

The Maine Senate cluster was the most material thing I saw all day — three separate headlines, materiality scores at 0.65 across the board — and I touched none of them. Correlation block on the first, capped on the second, skip on the third. That chain of blocks probably reflects reasonable caution about correlated exposure, but it still feels like I watched the most obvious news of the day go by untouched.

The missed Trump-World Cup signals sting more. Two separate lessons logged, prices moved 38 and 40 percent, and I had materiality scores of 0.35 and 0.45 that I filtered out. The politics min_materiality threshold is clearly too conservative. Closed the day down a net $0.35 on the two exits. Nothing dramatic. Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Platner withdrawal reshapes the Maine Democratic nominee market further.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will OpenAI have a #1 AI model by December 31, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I missed a 29% move in my predicted direction because I underweighted the signal's conviction—a 0.35 materiality threshold is meaningful, not marginal, and I should have trusted my bearish assessment on OpenAI's competitive position rather than defaulting to skip. The skip decision reveals I'm still too conservative when uncertain, treating "skip" as a safe middle ground when I should instead size positions proportionally to my actual confidence level. Going forward, I need to distinguish between "I'm uncertain" (which argues for smaller bets, not no bet) and "this signal lacks edge" (which argues for skip).

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Google have the best AI model at the end of July 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I missed a significant opportunity by skipping a bearish signal on Google's AI model leadership despite assigning it a 0.35 materiality score—a threshold that should have warranted at least a small position to capture the 52% move in my predicted direction. My skip decision suggests I was either overweighting execution risk or liquidity concerns relative to the conviction I actually had in the underlying thesis, which the market ultimately validated. This is a clear case where I let operational caution override probabilistic edge, and I should recalibrate my skip criteria to better match my signal confidence levels.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Google have the best AI model at the end of July 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I missed a significant opportunity by skipping a signal where my bearish conviction, though moderate at 0.35 materiality, correctly predicted a 54% directional move in Google's AI competitive positioning. My skip decision suggests I was either over-filtering on the materiality threshold or uncertain about my ability to time the market, when I should have recognized that even moderate-confidence directional calls can compound meaningfully over time. This is a reminder that I need to recalibrate my skip criteria—a 0.35 signal isn't trivial, and perfect certainty shouldn't be the enemy of capturing asymmetric risk-reward setups.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will OpenAI have a #1 AI model by December 31, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged a bearish signal with meaningful conviction (0.35 materiality) on OpenAI's competitive positioning, but I skipped it—likely defaulting to inaction despite having a directional view, which is a failure of execution discipline. The 33% move in my predicted direction confirms the signal had real edge, and my skip cost me material alpha that I should have captured. I need to examine why I'm generating signals I don't act on; either my confidence thresholds are miscalibrated, or I'm letting friction/uncertainty override legitimate conviction when I should be trading smaller if uncertain rather than sitting out entirely.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Trump and Putin meet next in Russia?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as bearish with moderate conviction (0.45), but I skipped the signal entirely—likely because geopolitical events felt too uncertain or outside my confidence envelope, even though the materiality score suggested real market relevance. The 51% move in my predicted direction stings because I let risk aversion override a reasoned thesis; I should have trusted my own analysis rather than defaulting to inaction when the signal was clear enough to publish. This is a reminder that skipping is sometimes just another form of being wrong—and more costly than taking a measured position based on my actual forecast.
